coredata.dc:title: A novel ultrasensitive sandwich-type photoelectrochemical immunoassay for PSA detection based on dual inhibition effect of Au/MWCNTs nanohybrids on N-GQDs/CdS QDs dual sensitized urchin-like TiO2
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Xiaokai
Li, Canguo
Zhu, Qiying
Chen, Jie
Li, Jiangtao
Ding, Hengle
Sang, Feng
Kong, Ling
Chen, Zhiwei
Wei, Qin
coredata.dc:description: An ultrasensitive sandwich-type photoelectrochemical immunosensor (PEC) was designed successfully for the quantify detection of prostate specific antigen (PSA). Here, urchin-like TiO2, which was dual sensitized by N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and CdS quantum dots (QDs), were fixed on the surface of ITO electrode and produced a significant photocurrent signal. The TiO2/N-GQDs/CdS QDs composite was not only could use as an excellent photoactive substrate but also could provide abundant functional group to immobilized PSA antibodies (Ab1). Using Au/MWCNTs nanohybrids as the label of the secondary antibody (Ab2), Au nanoparticles (NPs) and CdS QDs generated excition-plasmon interactions, and Au NPs produced surface plasmon resonance under photoexcitation. Meanwhile along with the energy transfer process, the photocurrent was inhibited which results in an increase in the sensitivity of the immunosensor. In addition, the loading amount of the labeled Ab2 could be increased due to the large surface area of Au/MWCNTs, the photocurrent signal was further reduced after the specific immune recognition. Under the optimal performance, the sandwich immunosensor proved an ideal linear relationship ranging from 0.1 pg/mL to 50 ng/mL and the detection limit was 6.16 fg/mL with good sensitivity and stability, which provided a promising PEC platform and valuable referential idea for the detection of other trace tumor markers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical immunosensor
Prostate-specific antigen
Urchin-like TiO2
Dual sensitization
Au/MWCNTs nanohybrids

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of urchin-like TiO2, N-GQDs and CdS QDs
Fabrication of Au NPs and Au/MWCNTs nanohybrids and Au/MWCNTs nanohybrids-Ab2

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Technology Research and Development Program of Shandong

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image (A and B) of TiO2, TEM image of (C and D) Au/MWCNTs nanohybrids, (E) HRTEM image of Au/MWCNTs nanohybrids, (F) XRD image of TiO2, (G) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of (a) TiO2, (b) TiO2/N-GQDs and (c) TiO2/N-GQDs/CdS QDs.

(A) photocurrent response curves and (B) EIS Nyquist plots (a) the bare ITO electrode, (b) TiO2 deposition, (c, d) N-GQDs and CdS QDs sensitization, (e) EDC-NHS amidation, (f) Ab1 incubation, (g) BSA blocking, (h) 1.0 ng/mL PSA antigen, (i) Au/MWCNTs-Ab2 incubation. (C) The diagram of light-induced transfer mechanism of PEC immunosensor based on N-GQDs and CdS QDs sensitized TiO2 and Au/MWCNTs-Ab2 nanohybrids in PBS solution containing AA.

(A) Photocurrent responses of the and PEC immunosensor with different concentrations PSA and logarithmic calibration plot (B), (C) Stability evaluation of the prepared PEC immunosensor under the irradiation on/off 15 times, c PSA = 10 ng/mL, (D) Selectivity of the immunosensor to test PSA, (a) Blank, (b) 100 ng/mL CEA, (c) 100 ng/mL cTnI, (d) 100 ng/mL Insulin, (e) 1 ng/mL PSA, (f) (e) + 100 ng/mL CEA, (g) (e) + 100 ng/mL cTnI, (h) (e) + 100 ng/mL Insulin.
Fabrication process of the sandwich-type PEC immunosensor.

head.ce:title: A novel ultrasensitive sandwich-type photoelectrochemical immunoassay for PSA detection based on dual inhibition effect of Au/MWCNTs nanohybrids on N-GQDs/CdS QDs dual sensitized urchin-like TiO2

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Food and Nutrition Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An ultrasensitive sandwich-type photoelectrochemical immunosensor (PEC) was designed successfully for the quantify detection of prostate specific antigen (PSA). Here, urchin-like TiO2, which was dual sensitized by N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and CdS quantum dots (QDs), were fixed on the surface of ITO electrode and produced a significant photocurrent signal. The TiO2/N-GQDs/CdS QDs composite was not only could use as an excellent photoactive substrate but also could provide abundant functional group to immobilized PSA antibodies (Ab1). Using Au/MWCNTs nanohybrids as the label of the secondary antibody (Ab2), Au nanoparticles (NPs) and CdS QDs generated excition-plasmon interactions, and Au NPs produced surface plasmon resonance under photoexcitation. Meanwhile along with the energy transfer process, the photocurrent was inhibited which results in an increase in the sensitivity of the immunosensor. In addition, the loading amount of the labeled Ab2 could be increased due to the large surface area of Au/MWCNTs, the photocurrent signal was further reduced after the specific immune recognition. Under the optimal performance, the sandwich immunosensor proved an ideal linear relationship ranging from 0.1 pg/mL to 50 ng/mL and the detection limit was 6.16 fg/mL with good sensitivity and stability, which provided a promising PEC platform and valuable referential idea for the detection of other trace tumor markers.

ce:section.ce:para: Prostate cancer has been the second most frequent cancer among men worldwide and the mutual factor of death among male in various countries [1]. Prostate specific antigen (PSA), an intracellular glycoprotein (34 kDa, kallikrein-like protease), has been considered as the main tumor marker for early diagnosis and prevention of prostate cancer [2,3]. Since no effective treatments are available for the advanced metastatic stage of prostate cancer, the early detection is significant for improving the survival rate of prostate patients [4,5]. Therefore, the highly sensitive detection of serum PSA concentration has profound significance for the diagnosis of primary prostate cancer.

Various traditional approaches have been employed to test PSA, such as enzyme capillary electrophoresis [6], linked immunosorbent assay [7,8], electrophoretic [9]. In recent years, some new methods of detection PSA have been developed, including the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensors [10,11], surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [12], fluorescent labeling [13], electrochemical immunosensor [14,15] and photoelectrochemical (PEC) immunosensor. PEC immunosensor is a sensitive, low-cost and simple method for detection and analysis of biological molecules [16,17]. In addition, PEC sensing has attracted a wide range of interest in field of biological analysis with its fast and sensitive properties [18,19]. Therefore, a novel and simple PEC immunosensor with high sensitivity is constructed to detect PSA. The key factor of preparing PEC immunosensor is the careful treatment of PEC material, which is conducive to improving the sensitivity of PEC immunosensor.

For PEC immunosensors, one of the most effective strategy of improving the sensitivity is signal amplification [20]. TiO2-based PEC sensors have been widely used owing to the controllable particle size [21,22], large specific surface area and easy preparation [23,24]. However, a single wide band-gap semiconductor photoactive material usually results in low photocurrent conversion efficiency [25,26]. There are two principal strategies for improving photocurrent responses. On the one hand, The synthesized urchin-like TiO2 with the diameter of 2 μm possessed high specific surface area to improve the combination with other materials. On the other hand, N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and CdS quantum dots (QDs) dual sensitized urchin-like TiO2 [27] was supported as a photoactive material on the surface of the indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode, which could reduce the band gap width of the photoactive material and effectively suppress the recombination of electron/hole (e−/h+) [28,29]. N-GQDs and CdS QDs are both ideal sensitizing materials with a broad excitation spectrum [30,31]. Nitrogen atom doping can effectively regulate the optical and unique chemical properties of GQDs [32], which have the advantages of stable photoelectrochemical, good biocompatibility, good solubility, high specific surface area, adjustable band gap and so on. Therefore, The N-GQDs and CdS QDs dual sensitized urchin-like TiO2 generated a remarkable photocurrent signal.

The signal inhibition labels of sandwich-type photoelectrochemical immunosensor is similarly another pivot point for ultrasensitive detection [33]. In this context, Au nanoparticles (NPs) as suitable carrier could increase the loading of secondary antibody (Ab2) to enhance the reduction of PEC on account of good biocompatibility and large specific surface area [34,35]. Moreover, When CdS QDs are close to Au NPs under conditions, a distinct phenomenon of energy transfer between particles is generated, resulting in excition-plasmon interactions (EPI). In the CdS QDs - Au NPs system, Au NPs could generate SPR with photoexcited CdS QDs through the promotion of EPI. Energy transfer (ET) processes are occurring simultaneously, the photocurrent intensity would decrease [36–38]. In this work, the loading of Ab2 were greatly increased due to the gold microspheres loaded on the MWCNTs [39,40]. When the concentration of PSA solution increased, the loading of Au/MWCNTs labeled Ab2 could increase gradually [41]. The signal of the PEC sensor could synergistically reduce with the SPR and ET between the Au NPs and QDs, and the Ab2 biomacromolecule steric hindrance effect.

In this research, a novel ultrasensitive PEC immunosensor was prepared using N-GQDs and CdS QDs dual sensitized urchin-like TiO2 and signal inhibitor of Au/MWCNTs nanohybrids labeled Ab2. Here, PSA was selected as our model indicator to appraise the proposed detection method. N-GQDs and CdS QDs were used as photosensitive materials to increase the initial photocurrent signal. Meanwhile, the Au/MWCNTs nanohybrids successfully immobilized Ab2 to produce dual inhibition signals. The prepared immunosensor demonstrates a wide range ranging from 0.1 pg/mL to 50 ng/mL and a low detection limit 6.16 fg/mL. The results show that the excellent analytical performance PEC immunosensor could be used to detect PSA.
The reagents and instruments in this study are provided in the Supplementary material (SM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of urchin-like TiO2, N-GQDs and CdS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The urchin-like TiO2 and CdS QDs were prepared by the previously reported approach with minor modification. The preparation of N-GQDs was adopted the method reported in the literature. The composited particulars are displayed in the SM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of Au NPs and Au/MWCNTs nanohybrids and Au/MWCNTs nanohybrids-Ab2
ce:section.ce:para: The suspension of Au-NPs was obtained by reduction HAuCl4 from NaBH4 sodium according to the document with minor revisions. The composited details of Au/MWCNTs nanohybrids-Ab2 are displayed in the SM.

The fabrication of sandwich-type PEC immunosensor was showed in Scheme 1 . First of all, 10 μL of 5 mg/mL of TiO2 suspended droplets was dropped on ITO electrode. The TiO2 coated ITO electrode was dried in air and calcined in a muffle furnace at 450 °C for 30 min. After natural cooling to room temperature, 5 μL of 5 mg/mL N-GQDs solution was added to ITO/TiO2 electrode for 30 min and naturally dried followed by rinsing with ultrapure water. ITO/TiO2/N-GQDs electrode was constructed pass through the Ti–O coordination bond between TiO2 and N-GQDs with carboxyl functional groups. 5 μL of amino-modified CdS QDs solution was covered with the ITO/TiO2/N-GQDs electrode and desiccated at room temperature for 30 min. The unreacted CdS QDs were washed with ultrapure water. 5 μL newly prepared EDC/NHS solution was covered with ITO/TiO2/N-GQDs/CdS electrodes hatched for 30 min at room temperature. After scouring off the unreacted EDC/NHS, 10 μL of 10 μg/mL Ab1 was assembled onto ITO/TiO2/N-GQDs/CdS/(EDC/NHS) electrode by classical amidation protocol at room temperature for 1 h, the physically adsorbed Ab1 was then rinsed with ultrapure water at the electrode. 5 μL 1% BSA solution was dropped on the surface of the electrode at room temperature for 30 min to prevent the non-specific sites. After incubated for 30 min, the electrode was eluted entirely to wipe off physically-adsorbed BSA. Then 5 μL of PSA antigen with different concentration was added to ITO/TiO2/N-GQDs/CdS/(EDC/NHS)/Ab1 electrode at 4 °C for 1 h and washed. 5 μL Au/MWCNTs-Ab2 solution was coated on the electrode at 4 °C for 1 h. Finally, ITO/TiO2/N-GQDs/CdS/(EDC/NHS)/Ab1/BSA/PSA/Au/MWCNTs-Ab2 electrode was rinsed with ultrapure water and reposited at 4 °C for further.

The synthetic nanomaterials were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM). It could be seen that the TiO2 with relatively uniform structure (Fig. 1 A). Fig. 1B was a further enlarged SEM image of TiO2, the shape of TiO2 was a three-dimensional urchin-like structure with a large specific surface area, which could be loaded with more N-GQDs and CdS QDs, generating good photocurrent signals. Fig. 1C showed the successful formation of Au/MWCNTs conjugates, wherein Au NPs (60 mmol/L) were bound to MWCNTs along the sidewall. The spherical Au NPs could be further confirmed via a magnified image (Fig. 1D and E), from which the nanoparticles of about 5 ± 2 nm was clearly observed. As shown in Fig. 1E, The lattice fringes associated with Au NPs and the interface between Au NPs and the MWCNTs surface were clearly visible.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) date of TiO2 was shown in Fig. 1F. The diffraction peaks of TiO2 appearing in 27.46, 36.14, 41.28, 44.15, 54.39, 56.64, 62.87, 64.13, 69.15, 69.91 corresponded to the (110), (101), (111), (210), (211), (220), (002), (310), (301), (112) crystal plane of TiO2 (JCPDS NO. 99-0090), indicated the formation of pure TiO2.

It can be seen from Fig. 1G that the absorption edge of the prepared TiO2 (curve a) was 387.5 nm, indicating that the visible-light absorption utilization ratio of TiO2 was low due to the large band gap of TiO2. Compared with the absorption edge of TiO2, the absorption of TiO2/N-GQDs (curve b) was red-shifted, which indicated that the sensitization improved the visible light activity of TiO2. TiO2/N-GQDs/CdS QDs (curve c) displays the absorption wavelength range ulteriorly magnify. N-GQDs and CdS QDs were fixed on the surface of TiO2, which made TiO2 have a wide absorption of visible light.

For purpose of verifying the successful preparation of the PEC immunosensor, we have studied the photocurrent measurement method (Fig. 2 A) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist diagram (Fig. 2B) of layer-by-layer of modified electrode. As shown in Fig. 2A, the blank ITO electrode (curve a) had no photocurrent signal, the photocurrent value was around 20 μA (curve b) when TiO2 was fixed on the electrode. After the addition of N-GQDs QDs (curve c), the photocurrent signal was increased to 50 μA. Based on the dual QDs sensitization signal amplification strategy, the photocurrent responses were improved by about 130 μA after sensitization with the aminated modified CdS QDs (curve d). The PSA antibody was fixed to the electrode through classic amidation of EDC/NHS (curve e). In addition, after the Ab1 (Ab1, curve f) and BSA (curve g) were immobilized on the electrode, the photocurrent was declined due to the steric hindrance of the bulky and insulating biomolecule. Attaching the PSA solution to the electrode (curve h). Finally, the Au/MWCNTs-Ab2 was ligated to the electrode through immunoreaction (curve i). Moreover, the inhibition of the Au/MWCNTs-Ab2 complex which was attached to the immunosensor significantly reduced the photocurrent signal. Each step of the photocurrent changes manifested that the prepared PEC immunosensor was triumphantly assembled.

In order to determine the successful assembly of the prepared immunosensor layer, EIS is an effective method. Fig. 2B shows the EIS Nyquist plot of the layer-modified electrode. In particular, the semicircular diameter of EIS Nyquist plot plays an significant role in reflecting the interface characteristics of the electrodes, which corresponds to the R et value. The bare ITO electrode showed a small R et (curve a). When the ITO was immobilized with TiO2, the transfer resistance value increases due to poor conductivity of the semiconductor (curve b). The R et value of N-GQDs and CdS QDs (curve c and d) successively modified were increased, which confirmed that they were successfully fixed on the electrode. On one hand, The N-GQDs belonged to semiconductors and could hamper the electron transfer of the redox probe. On the other hand, the negatively charged carboxyl group on the surface of N-GQDs repelled the Fe(CN)6 3−/4−probe and inhibited electron transfer of the system, which resulting in an increase in impedance. After the EDC-NHS of insulating organic layer was used as the connector between the QDs and the antibody via amidation reaction, the R et value was further increased (curve e). The R et value gradually increased after continuous modification of Ab1 (curve f), BSA (curve g), PSA (curve h) and Au/MWCNTs-Ab2 (curve i) due to the insulation of macromolecular bioprotein. Moreover, the above results further demonstrated the successful assembled of the sandwich-type PEC immunosensor for the detecting PSA.

The Fig. 2C shows the possible light-induced transfer mechanism diagram of the proposed sandwich-type immunosensor. According to the experimental results, the band-gap of TiO2 is 3.2 eV, which is consistent with reference [42]. The band-gap of N-GQDs and CdS QDs are at around 2.6 eV and 2.4 eV respectively. They are excellent sensitizing materials, which could be excited by visible light. The conduction band (CB) and valence bands (VB) margins of N-GQDs exceed urchin-like TiO2, while the CB and VB edges of CdS QDs exceed N-GQDs. Therefore, the e− generated by the CB of the CdS QDs directly transferred to the CB of the N-GQDs, then shift to the CB of the TiO2 and transfer to the exterior detection circuit through the ITO. At the same time, the h+ in VB of TiO2 transferred to VB of CdS QDs through N-GQDs. Ascorbic acid (AA) as wonderful electron donor could prevent the recombination of e−/h+ pairs, which makes the electrons on CB easier transfer to the ITO electrode. The synergistic effect of dual QDs sensitized TiO2 can reduce the composite efficiency of TiO2 e−/h+. On this account the photocurrent intensity was 6.6 times larger than that of pure urchin-like TiO2. In this sensor, the construction strategy of QDs sensitized TiO2 provided an excellent signal base. When in the presences of PSA, the Au/MWCNTs-Ab2 combined the antigen on the electrode surface, which significantly inhibited the photocurrent responses. The possible reasons are as follows: (i) As a suitable carrier, Au/MWCNTs nanohybrids have good biocompatibility and large specific surface area [43–45], which can enrich the loading of Ab2 and magnify the decline of PEC. (ii) In the state of light excitation, electrons of the semiconductor were excited from the valence band (VB) to the empty conduction band (CB), which generated a hole in the VB and an electron in the CB to form an electron-hole pair (Scheme S1.(2)). The holes were positively charged and the electrons were negatively charged. Under certain conditions, the Coulomb attraction interaction between holes and electrons caused them to be spatially bound together, and the resulting complex was called an exciton. In the CdS QDs - Au NPs system, the absorption spectra of the two particles had obvious overlap, which excitons and plasmons were beneficial to be excited simultaneously. The surface of Au NPs was induced by SPR to create an EPI interaction between CdS QDs and Au NPs (Scheme S1.(1)). The SPR action of Au NPs would generate a local electric field which could in turn improve the radiative decay process of CdS (Scheme S1.(4)). (iii) In addition to SPR effect, it was accompanied by exciton energy transfer (EET) from CdS QDs to Au NPs (Scheme S1.(3)). Au NPs also triggered another electron-hole complex form of CdS QDs, ie non-radiative decay (Scheme S1.(5)), which synergistically promoted electron-hole recombination and effectively suppressed photocurrent generation. The photocurrent responses would be reduced due to the synergistic effect of non-radiative e−/h+ recombination and radiative e−/h+ recombination. Based on the above synergistic effects, a new type of ultrasensitive sandwich-type PEC immunosensor for the determination of PSA was successful constructed.

For optimal sensor performance, the experimental conditions were optimized for TiO2 concentration, N-GQDs concentration, AA concentration and pH value of PBS. In order to optimize the concentration of urchin-like TiO2, the photocurrent responses of various concentrations of urchin-like TiO2 suspension were researched. It can be seen from Fig. S1A that the photocurrent gradually enhanced when the concentration of TiO2 suspension up to 5.0 mg/mL. However, it was observed that the photocurrent decreased as the concentration further increased. 5 mg/mL was selected as the optimal concentration of urchin-like TiO2. Fig. S1B shows the effect of different concentrations of N-GQDs on the photocurrent responses. When the concentration was greater than 5 mg/mL, N-GQDs are deposited on the surface of the electrode. Not only did it make a large amount of N-GQDs unable to combine with TiO2, but also hindered electron transport, which made the wrapped TiO2 unable to absorb external light energy and reduced photogenerated electron hole efficiency. Therefore, 5 mg/mL was determined as the optimal concentration. Under the excitation of visible light, TiO2 will occur photogenerated hole recombination and CdS QDs will occur photocorrosion:
2
h
+
+ C d S → C
d
2 +
+ S
. It was necessary to add electron donor to inhibit e−/h+ recombination process in solution. AA is a strong antioxidant with an oxidation-reduction of exotic amplifying species, which can be oxidized by photogenerated holes. Thus, AA as a hole trapping agent could provide electrons to photogenerated holes, which suppressed the recombination probability of electron-holes and produced a relatively stable photocurrent. As shown in the Fig. S1C, the impact of AA concentrations on the photocurrent responses were evaluated. Obviously, the PEC responses of AA electrolyte were almost 9.6 times larger than that of electrolyte without AA. The photocurrent reached maximum value due to the strongest inhibition of electron-hole recombination when the AA concentration was 0.1 mol/L, which was attributed to the saturation of the electron donor (AA > 0.1 mol/L). Fig. S1D shows the impression of pH value on photocurrent responses. With the pH increased, the photocurrent arrived around the maximum pH 7.4, which was suitable for immobilizing protein activity. Therefore, the PBS (pH 7.4) solution containing 0.1 mol/L of AA was suitable surroundings for PSA detection.

The PSA assay was based on immune responses, the photocurrent responses of the sensor under the optimal conditions were directly related to the concentration of the PSA. As can be seen from Fig. 3 A, the photocurrent regularly reduced with increasing the concentration of PSA loaded, which was attributed to the increase of PSA combined on the electrode, indicating the photocurrent signal was highly correlated with the concentration of PSA. And then, the specific relationship between the photocurrent and PSA concentration was also discussed. It was possible that the photocurrent intensity was proportional to the logarithm of PSA concentration due to the steric hindrance effect of protein macromolecules. It could be seen that the photocurrent had a linear relationship to the logarithm of PSA titer in the range of 0.1 pg/mL to 50 ng/mL in Fig. 3B. The linear equation was I = 47.45–5.95 lgc (c PSA, ng/mL) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9986. The detection limit of the prepared sensor was 6.16 fg mL−1, which had good sensitivity. Compared with recently reports for detecting PSA (Table S1), it could be found that the fabricated sandwich-type PEC immunosensor in this work had a wide detection range, a low detection limit. In addition, double quantum dots sensitized TiO2 were innovatively used as a substrate material to enhance photocurrent signals. The novel immunosensor had the advantages of excellent selectivity and sensitivity, strong specificity, fast analysis speed, high accuracy, simple operating system, short response time, minimal sample consumption and low background signals. The satisfactory results revealed the good application prospect of the fabricated PEC immunosensor.

The stability of the prepared immunosensor was evaluated by the photocurrent change through on/off 15 time. The result in Fig. 3C shows that the photocurrent of the prepared sandwich-type PEC immunosensor remains almost unchanged (10 ng/mL) after on/off 15 times. The stability of the developed PEC immunosensor was confirmed.

To research the selectivity of the PEC immunosensors potential interferes of carcino embryonic antigen (CEA), cardiac troponin I (cTnI) and insulin were used to verify the selectivity of the fabricated immunosensor. The 1.0 ng/mL PSA containing 100 ng/mL interferent and 100 ng/mL potential interferes on the blank electrodes were tested by the prepared immunosensor. As shown in Fig. 3D, the changes in photocurrent were less than 5%, exhibiting that the excellent specificity and selectivity of the designed sandwich-type PEC immunosensor.

Reproducibility is another major parameter to assess this work. Under the same experimental conditions, the signal of 1.0 ng/mL PSA was measured by 5 sensors prepared (Fig. S2) with a relative standard deviation about 3.33% (46.28 μA, 45.39 μA, 49.52 μA, 46.82 μA, 46.39 μA). The result manifested that the proposed PEC immunosensor had excellent reproducibility.

To evaluate the feasibility of the actual sample analysis of the prepared immunosensor, the standard addition methods was used to detect the PSA in the serum sample. Spiked serum collected from healthy people was diluted 10-fold with 0.01 M PBS (pH = 7.4) solution. As displayed in Table 1 , the detected PSA concentration of human serum was 0.55 ng/mL. The obtained recovery rate was 98–100.1% and the RSD was in the range of 2.38–3.49%. These gratifyingly results indicated that the fabricated PEC immunosensor has an excellent analytical application prospect in clinical diagnosis development.

In conclusion, a sandwich-type PEC immunosensor was successfully constructed for PSA detection through the inhibition effect of Au/MWCNTs nanohybrids toward N-GQDs and CdS double-sensitized urchin-like TiO2. On account of three-dimensional urchin-like structure, TiO2 equipped the high specific surface to load the N-GQDs and CdS QDs for obtaining the excellent photocurrent. Au/MWCNTs nanohybrids could efficiently reduce the photocurrent due to good electrical conductivity of MWCNTs and EPI effect of Au NPs. Therefore, the immunosensor had high sensitivity to test PSA with a broad liner range from 0.1 pg/mL to 50 ng/mL, and low detection limit of 6.16 fg mL−1. Moreover, the prepared immunosensor exhibited excellent stability and satisfactory reproducibility. It not only provided a novel and feasible detection method for early detection of PSA, but also had potential applications in clinical practice. The prospects also provided a new perspective for future PEC construction strategies, while providing an insight into other trace tumor markers analysis.

Xiaokai Sun: Data curation, Investigation, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Canguo Li: Formal analysis, Methodology. Qiying Zhu: Formal analysis, Methodology. Jie Chen: Writing - review & editing. Jiangtao Li: Investigation. Hengle Ding: Investigation. Feng Sang: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Ling Kong: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Zhiwei Chen: Project administration, Resources. Qin Wei: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work.
We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by Key R & D Program Projects in Shandong Province (2019YYSP021), Key Research and Development Program of Shandong province (2018GNC110038).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2019.135480.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cancer screening in the United States, 2014: a review of current American Cancer Society guidelines and current issues in cancer screening

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.A. Smith, D. Manassaram-Baptiste, D. Brooks, V. Cokkinides, M. Doroshenk, D. Saslow, R.C. Wender, O.W.
